coredata.dc:title: Green fluorescent carbon quantum dots as a label-free probe for rapid and sensitive detection of hematin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Jia Yu
Han, Lei
Liu, Shi Gang
Ju, Yan Jun
Gao, Xin
Li, Nian Bing
Luo, Hong Qun
coredata.dc:description: Hematin is an oxidized form of heme, and the abnormal levels of hematin in the human body can lead to various inflammatory lesions. Hence, there is still a need to establish a rapid, sensitive and efficient method for hematin detection. Herein, the green fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are synthesized by using L-cysteine and hydrogen peroxide as precursors. The synthesized CQDs exhibit some fascinating characters including excellent water solubility, high fluorescence quantum yield, and good stability in a broad pH range of 7.0–11.0 and high ionic strength solution. Excitingly, the fluorescence of CQDs can be rapidly and selectively quenched by hematin via the inner filter effect. Moreover, the detection of hematin by the CQDs fluorescent probe shows a good linearity in the concentration range of 0.5–30 μM with a minimum detection limit of 0.1 μM. Finally, the proposed approach is successfully applied to detect hematin in human blood samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hematin
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence quenching
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assay of Hematin in a Buffer Solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Typical TEM image of CQDs. The inset displays the HRTEM image (top) and the size distribution of CQDs obtained from TEM image analysis (bottom). (B) XPS spectrum of CQDs. (C) The high-resolution spectrum of C1s. (D) The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s.
(A) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CQDs. Insets show the photographs of CQDs under visible light and 365 nm UV light. (B) The FT-IR spectrum of CQDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of L-Cys after heating for 12 h with the pH 9.5, (a) only L-Cys, (b) L-Cys and NaClO, (c) L-Cys and H2O2, (d) L-Cys heated by reflow. (B) Photographs of the products of L-Cys, L-Cys with NaClO, L-Cys with H2O2 and L-Cys heated by reflow under visible light and 365 nm UV light, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs after addition of various concentrations of hematin. (B) Fluorescence intensity changes of CQDs in the presence of various concentrations of hematin. Inset is the linear relationship of (F 0-F)/F 0 vs concentration of hematin ranging from 0 to 100 μM, where F 0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity of CQDs before and after addition of hematin, respectively. Conditions: CQDs, 10% (v/v); BR buffer (pH 7.4); excitation, 410 nm.

(A) Fluorescence quenching efficiency (F 0-F)/F 0 of CQDs in the presence of different metal ions and amino acids. F 0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the absence and presence of metal ions and amino acids, respectively. The concentrations of metal ions, amino acids, Hb, and hematin are 50 μM, 100 mΜ, 100 μg/mL, and 25 μM, respectively. (B) The concentrations of anions and hematin are 100 and 25 μM, respectively. Conditions: BR buffer (pH 7.4); CQDs, 10% (v/v); excitation, 410 nm; emission, 505 nm.

Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of fluorescent CQDs and detection principle for hematin. Insets: photographs of the aqueous solution of CQDs in the absence (a, b) and presence (c, d) of hematin under visible light (a, c) and 365 nm UV light (b, d).

head.ce:title: Green fluorescent carbon quantum dots as a label-free probe for rapid and sensitive detection of hematin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Tiansheng Road, BeiBei District, Chongqing 400715, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hematin is an oxidized form of heme, and the abnormal levels of hematin in the human body can lead to various inflammatory lesions. Hence, there is still a need to establish a rapid, sensitive and efficient method for hematin detection. Herein, the green fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are synthesized by using L-cysteine and hydrogen peroxide as precursors. The synthesized CQDs exhibit some fascinating characters including excellent water solubility, high fluorescence quantum yield, and good stability in a broad pH range of 7.0–11.0 and high ionic strength solution. Excitingly, the fluorescence of CQDs can be rapidly and selectively quenched by hematin via the inner filter effect. Moreover, the detection of hematin by the CQDs fluorescent probe shows a good linearity in the concentration range of 0.5–30 μM with a minimum detection limit of 0.1 μM. Finally, the proposed approach is successfully applied to detect hematin in human blood samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Hematin is a Fe(III) protoporphyrin IX ligand that has a hydroxyl group bound to Fe(III) and is also an oxidized form of heme. Heme (Fe(II) protoporphyrin IX) is a hemoglobin, myoglobin, and cytochrome prosthetic group [1]. When hemoglobin is released from red blood cells, Fe(II) is oxidized into Fe(III) form and binds a hydroxyl group to form hematin [2]. Hematin has been shown to be a toxic substance in vivo and can dissolve human red cell membranes and other cell types [3–5]. Furthermore, hematin is more adherent to sickle cells, white blood cells, and endothelial cells because it is easy to aggregate in aqueous solution and strong adhesion to the surface of red blood cells at physiological pH [6,7]. In addition, abnormal levels of hematin in the human body can lead to various pathologies, including renal failure, mitochondrial dysfunction, vascular disease, and immune-mediated diseases [8–11]. Therefore, it is important to establish a reliable and rapid method for determining hematin.

Currently, a series of methods for detecting hematin have been developed, such as electrochemical methods [12,13], mass spectrometry [14], fluorescence spectroscopy [15,16], absorption spectroscopy [17], and high performance liquid chromatography [18], but these methods mostly measure total heme in erythrocytes. In order to achieve a single detection of hematin in red blood cells, Aich et al. established an approach to determine hematin concentration in healthy human red blood cells based on enzyme-catalyzed chemiluminescence [19]. Although this method is capable of determining hematin in red blood cells, it requires lengthy procedures and expensive instruments for measurement, and takes time. Ji et al. reported an approach for detecting hematin concentration in human red blood cells using boron-doped graphene quantum dots (B-GQDs) as fluorescent probes without separating hematin from hemoglobin [20]. However, the preparation of B-GQD requires a good control of voltage and synthesis conditions, which is more complicated. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as an important part of nanomaterial, have many advantages, such as excellent optical properties, low toxicity, good water solubility, low cost, good biocompatibility, and environmental friendliness. [21–23]. Due to its good performance, Zhang et al. proposed a method by using CQDs as fluorescent probes to determine hematin based on the inner filter effect (IFE), in which CQDs were prepared using ethanol and p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) as precursors [24]. Nevertheless, PABA is classified by the International Agency for Research on Cancer as category III carcinogen and has moderate toxicity, which hinders their broad applications.

Herein, we propose a simple one-pot synthesis of CQDs with strong green fluorescence using L-cysteine (L-Cys) and hydrogen peroxide as precursors. The as-prepared CQDs have high quantum yield, good water solubility, and good stability. Meanwhile, the CQDs as a fluorescent probe can achieve rapid detection of hematin with a reaction time of only 1 min. Scheme 1 illustrates the synthetic process of CQDs and the method of determining hematin. Because of the large overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of hematin and the excitation spectrum of CQDs, the IFE can make the hematin effectively quench the fluorescence of CQDs. Moreover, the method has been used to determine hematin in hemolysate with satisfactory outcomes, which means that the synthesized fluorescent probe has a promising application for detecting hematin in red cells.

L-cysteine, H2O2 (30 wt%), hemoglobin (Hb), glutathione (GSH), glycine (Gly), Tyrosine (Tyr), and ascorbic acid (AA) were from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd., (Shanghai, China). Hematin was purchased from Adamas Reagent, Ltd., (Shanghai, China). The other reagents used in the experiment were analytical reagent grade and were used without any further purification. And all solutions were configured using ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm). Human red cell samples were from Southwest University Hospital (Chongqing, China).

Fluorescence spectra were tested on an F-2700 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) pictures were obtained from the JEM-2100F (JEOL, Japan). Ultraviolet visible absorption spectra were obtained from a UV–vis 2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out with the Escalab 250XI (ThermoFisher, USA). Fluorescence lifetime decays were collected with an Edinburgh FLS980. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured by using a Bruker IFS 113v spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). All pH values were determined by a FiveEasy Plus FE20 (METTLER TOLEDO, Ltd., China).

Briefly, 0.3635 g of L-Cys, 3.3 mL of NaOH (1.0 M), 300 μL of H2O2 (30 wt%), and 11.4 mL of ultrapure water were added to a 20 mL sample vial with a lid (the pH of the mixture is 9.50). Subsequently, the vial was allowed to stir at 90 °C for 12 h, and the colorless mixed solution gradually turned to bright yellow. Finally, the as-prepared CQDs solution was purified via a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cutoff 500 Da) for 12 h with ultrapure water, and the product was collected and stored in a fridge at 4 °C.

To obtain red blood cell hemolysate, we refer to the method proposed by Aich and coworkers [19]. Blood samples taken by male and female healthy volunteers at Southwest University Hospital. For the preparation of hemolysate, 20 mL of physiological saline was added to 2 mL of blood sample and shaken at a small amplitude to make the suspension uniform, then the suspension was centrifuged (3000 rpm, 5 min) and decanted the supernatant. The blood sample was washed with physiological saline three times and further centrifuged (3000 rpm, 10 min) to prepare compressed red blood cells. Subsequently, 25 mL of ultrapure water was added in the compressed red blood cells. The red blood cells rupture and heme in the form of hematin was released from red blood cells under the effect of the osmotic pressure difference between water and red blood cells. Finally, the suspension was then centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 15 min) to remove the cell membranes that were pressed against the wall of the tube. The obtained hemolysate was stored at 4 °C for further experimentation. Prior to detect hematin in red blood cells, the hemolysate was diluted 100 times with ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of Hematin in a Buffer Solution
ce:section.ce:para: For the determination of hematin, 50 μL of CQDs, 440 μL of Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (pH 7.4), and 10 μL of hematin solution with various concentrations were added sequentially to a 1.5 mL plastic centrifuge tube, then the mixture was shaked for 1 min. Finally, at the excitation wavelength of 410 nm the fluorescence spectra were collected.

The quantum yield (QY) of the CQDs was tested using the widely accepted process [25]. In this paper, fluorescein (QY = 95% in 0.1 M NaOH) was used as a reference for CQDs. In order to minimize the reabsorption effect, five concentrations of CQDs and fluorescein solutions with the absorbance of less than 0.05 at 410 nm were prepared. And then the integrated fluorescence intensity as a function of the corresponding absorbance value was used to chart. Finally, the QY of CQDs was estimated according to the following formula:
(1)
φ x
=
φ st
K x
/
K st
η x
/
η st
2
where φ is the quantum yield, K is the slope, and η is the refractive index (1.33 for water). The subscripts “x” and “st” represent sample and standard, respectively.

L-cysteine is a common amino acid in the body and contains carbon, nitrogen, and sulfhydryl groups. Hydrogen peroxide is a common oxidant and oxygen donor. What is important is that they are inexpensive and environmentally friendly materials. Therefore, the use of L-Cys and hydrogen peroxide to synthesize CQDs through a one-pot hydrothermal process is a cost-effective method. CQDs preparation process is illustrated in Scheme 1. The mechanism of CQDs formation may be that during the heating process, L-Cys is disintegrated and polymerized into small dots as carbon nuclei, which then aggregate to form CQDs, which is similar to the reported literature [26].

The TEM image displays that the CQDs have good dispersibility (Fig. 1A), and the average particle size is 4.4 nm, which is mostly distributed in the range of 3.0–5.0 nm (Fig. 1A, inset of the bottom right). The HRTEM image presented in the right top illustration of Fig. 1A suggests that the as-synthesized CQDs have high crystallinity and resolvable lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, matching the lattice constant in the (100) plane of graphite carbon [27], which is similar to the previous reports on CQDs [24,28].

The surface composition of CQDs can be researched by XPS spectroscopy. Fig. 1B displays the existence of C, N, O, and S elements in CQDs with atomic percentages of approximately 72.96%, 16.36%, 10.36%, and 0.66%, respectively. Fig. 1C reveals that the high-resolution XPS peaks for C1s indicate the existence of CO (288.0 eV), CN (286.1 eV), and CC (284.6 eV) [21]. The XPS spectrum of N1s indicates the presence of NH (402.7 eV) and CNC (399.6 eV) (Fig. 1D) [26]. High-resolution XPS spectrum of S2p shows two peaks at 171.3 and 168.6 eV (Fig. S1) [29], which belong to –CSO4– and –CSO3– bonds. These results indicate that there are more oxygen-containing groups, nitrogen-containing groups, and trace sulfur-containing groups on the surface of the synthesized CQDs.

Fig. 2A shows the fluorescence spectrum, UV–vis absorption spectrum and optical pattern of the CQDs. The CQDs have a characteristic absorption peak at 288 nm and should belong to n-π* transition of the CO bond [26]. At 410 nm excitation (Fig. 2A, curve Ex), the maximum emission of CQDs is at 505 nm (Fig. 2A, curve Em). The solution of CQDs is faint yellow under natural light and emits bright green fluorescence under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (inset of Fig. 2A). Furthermore, the solution of CQDs was compared to an unheated mixed solution that did not show any significant UV–vis absorption peak and fluorescence emission peak at 288 and 505 nm, respectively (Fig. S2A, curve a, Fig. S2B, curve a). Fig. S3 reveals the fluorescence emission spectrum of CQDs under various excitation wavelengths. As the excitation wavelength is changed from 370 to 460 nm, the emission wavelength of CQDs almost does not shift. The excitation-independent fluorescence behavior of CQDs, may be related to the effect of the narrow size distribution and surface passivation function of the CQDs [30]. All results showed that the strong fluorescent CQDs have been successfully prepared.

Meanwhile, we also performed FT-IR spectral characterization of CQDs. As indicated in Fig. 2B, the characteristic absorption peaks of CQDs at 3424 and 1125 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of (NH, OH) and CO, respectively. Therefore, a hydroxyl group exists on the surface of the CQDs. The stretching vibration peaks of CO, CC, and CN are located at 1623, 1599, and 1406 cm−1, respectively, indicating that CQDs have an amide bond. The weak peak at 606 cm−1 is the bending vibration of CS. These results indicate that the hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the CQDs surface may be from the carbon precursor L-Cys, which is compatible with the outcomes obtained from XPS spectrum. When excited at 410 nm, the fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs was estimated to be approximately 43.9% using fluorescein in NaOH (0.1 M) solution as a standard (Fig. S4).

A range of experimental parameters were optimized to obtain the strongest fluorescence intensities of CQDs, including the concentration ratio of L-Cys to H2O2, pH value, and synthesis time. Fig. S5A reveals that the optimum concentration ratio of L-Cys to H2O2 is 1:1. The optimum pH value (Fig. S5B) and preparation time are 9.50 and 12.0 h (Fig. S5C), respectively. The CQDs were synthesized under the aforesaid optimal conditions and applied to all subsequent experiments.

To explore the role of hydrogen peroxide in the synthesis reaction, we conducted a series of studies. As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence spectra show that under the same experimental conditions, green fluorescent CQDs emitting at 505 nm can be prepared only when hydrogen peroxide is added to the reaction or a reflux device is used, but the former has the strongest fluorescence intensity, and the photos in visible light and ultraviolet light are also consistent with this result (Fig. 3B). When sodium hypochlorite was used instead of hydrogen peroxide, green carbon dots cannot be prepared. Therefore, we speculated that the reactive oxygen species provided by hydrogen peroxide reacts with cysteine and plays a crucial role in the preparation of strong green fluorescent CQDs.

The synthesized CQDs shows good stability. In the BR solution with pH 6.0–11.0, the fluorescence intensities of CQDs didn't change significantly (Fig. S6A). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in various levels of NaCl solution did not change significantly, and CQDs can maintain the fluorescence in the high ionic strength solution for at least 2 h (Fig. S6B). In addition, CQDs are stable for more than 2 months in a 4 °C refrigerator. The good stability of CQDs indicates that it possesses great application prospect as a fluorescent probe to detect targets in complex matrices.

To improve the detection sensitivity of hematin, it is necessary to optimize the detection conditions, such as the pH of the solution and the interaction time. Fig. S7A displays that the fluorescence intensities of the probe solution remained almost constant in the pH range of 7.0–10.5 and the degree of quenching of the probe by the hematin remained essentially unchanged. This suggests that the detection process can be carried out in the alkaline range. Therefore, the physiological pH (7.4) was selected to obtain the best test results.

To achieve the optimum incubation time for hematin detection, the fluorescence intensities of hematin-containing CQDs probe solutions at different incubation times were measured. Obviously, the reaction is complete within 1 min (Fig. S7B). Simultaneously, it was discussed whether the incubation temperature will affect the determination of hematin. With the reaction temperature altered from 20 to 80 °C, the relative fluorescence intensity of the CQDs hardly changed (Fig. S8). All of the results mentioned above demonstrated that hematin determination with a reaction time of 1 min can be selected to achieve the rapid detection of hematin at the room temperature.

Under the optimum conditions, the sensitivity of the fluorescent CQDs probe to hematin determination was studied. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs at 505 nm gradually reduces as the hematin concentration increases from 0 to 100 μM (Fig. 4A). And the hematin level in the range of 0.5–30 μM is directly proportional to the fluorescence quenching efficiency (Fig. 4B). The linear regression equation can be expressed as (F 0 − F)/F 0 = 0.02204C + 0.01522 (R 2 = 0.998), where F
0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity of CQDs before and after the addition of hematin, respectively, and C is the concentration of hematin. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.1 μM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Meanwhile, UV–vis absorption spectra of the mixtures of various concentrations of hematin and CQDs were carried out (Fig. S9A). As the hematin concentration increases, the absorption peak at 385 nm gradually increases. The dependence of absorbance on the hematin concentration is linear in the range of 4–80 μM (R 2 = 0.9993) (Fig. S9B). This result indicates that the fluorescence method has a lower detection limit and is more sensitive than the UV–vis absorption method. In addition, the absorption peak is susceptible to interference from other substances in the ultraviolet spectral region.

To assess the specificity of the probe for detecting hematin, we studied the possible molecules, metal ions, and anions contained in the complex biological matrices. When different ions and molecules were added to the CQDs solution, their fluorescence intensities at 505 nm were recorded. Fig. 5A shows the fluorescence intensity is significantly decreased only when hematin is added to the CQDs solution, while adding other molecules or metal ions does not substantially affect the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs solution. The effect of anions on hematin detection was also explored. As shown in Fig. 5B, the anions do not affect the detection of hematin. The results show that the method has high selectivity for the detection of hematin.

The mechanism of CQDs detection of hematin was explored by UV spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, cyclic voltammetry, etc. As shown in Fig. S10, The UV–vis absorption spectrum of hematin has a broad overlap with the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CQDs, which means that fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or IFE maybe result in decreased fluorescence [22,31,32]. The fluorescence decay time of CQDs shows a little change ranging from 6.45 to 6.38 after the addition of hematin, which manifests that there was no energy transfer between CQDs and hematin. This is consistent with the literature reports (Fig. S11) [33–35]. In order to investigate whether electron transfer induced fluorescence quenching, the electrochemical properties of CQDs were studied by cyclic voltammetry (CV). The lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy levels of CQDs were estimated. As displayed in Fig. S12A, the E red (reduction potential of CQDs) was estimated to be −0.58 eV, and the E g (the energy band gap that is calculated from the absorption edge in the UV–vis spectrum of CQDs) was determined to be 3.78 eV (Fig. S12B). The E HOMO and E LUMO of CQDs calculated according to the empirical formula are −7.60 and −3.82 eV, respectively [36,37]. Meanwhile, the E HOMO and E LUMO of hematin can be estimated as −5.06 and −2.15 eV, respectively [20]. Obviously, no electron transfer occurs between CQDs and hematin (Fig. S13). In addition, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of hematin on CQDs does not increase with increasing temperature (Fig. S8). This also shows that the quenching mechanism is not dynamic quenching but should be static quenching [38]. The above results exclude the energy transfer and electron transfer and also rule out the possibility of dynamic quenching. The excitation and emission spectra of CQDs have broad overlap with the ultraviolet spectrum of hematin. Therefore, the quenching mechanism by hematin can be deduced as IFE.

To assess the utility of this process for detecting hematin, the concentration of hematin in the hemolysate was detected. The results based on three repeated measurements are summarized in Table S1. Examination of this table shows that the estimated value by the process is compatible with the spiked value of the hematin concentration. The recovery of the sample is in the range of 99.6% to 104.0%, and the relative standard deviation ranges from 0.78% to 4.18%. The experimental results show that the method has a good reliability, accuracy and repeatability, indicating that this method has great potential application.

In summary, we successfully prepared and characterized carbon quantum dots with strong green fluorescence emission by a simple hydrothermal method using L-Cys and hydrogen peroxide as precursors. The active oxygen provided by hydrogen peroxide plays an important role in the preparation of strong green fluorescent CQDs. The prepared CQDs have a size range of 3.0–5.0 nm, a fluorescence quantum yield of 43.9%, and good water solubility. Also, the CQDs exhibit excellent stability in pH ranging from 7.0 to 11.0 and high ionic strength solutions. Using the CQDs as the fluorescent probe, a simple method for high sensitive, selective, and fast detection of hematin in human blood samples was established based on the IFE. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs was linear with the hematin concentration in the range of 0.5–30 μM and the detection limit was 0.1 μM. Especially, this label-free detection method is simple to operate, green, and inexpensive, and has been successfully used for determining hematin in human blood samples, indicating good prospects for disease diagnosis and clinical examination.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675131, 21273174) and the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (No. CSTC-2015jcyjB50001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.01.001.
